In short, it's there to make your code a bit safer.
There's one thing to watch out for though. If you write it like this:
and everything underneath it (its components and child components) gets set to StrictMode.
So let's quickly walk through what actually happens once you use
. We'll write a simple example for it:

See the Pen (130) React useLog by Ray (@hsiangfeng) on CodePen.
In this example we've only got one component, and it doesn't use any lifecycle stuff, so you won't see any weird error messages. But the moment you try doing something with
, the results get a little different:
See the Pen (131) React StrictMode by Ray (@hsiangfeng) on CodePen.
Open the console and you'll notice
ran twice — normally it should only run once. This happens because
checks whether your component has any unsafe lifecycle behavior, so it deliberately runs things twice. The idea is to make it easier for you to spot problems.
